data.title: AMDY: Still A Buy, But Expectations Should Be Lower
data.description:
I have been issuing Buys for a lot of covered call strategies in the current market conditions based on the simple expectation of a rangebound market ahead. I had issued a Buy for JEPI recently, for instance, and that was based on a rangebound outlook. On one hand, slowing growth and sticky inflation seem to cap runaway rallies we saw in 2025. Then there are valuation pressures in the market cap dominating AI segment where long duration growth assumptions are being challenged. Having said so, we have had meaningful corrections already from the top (toward the end of 2025) and with secular demand tailwinds from AI, the downside from here looks limited too. The high odds of a flat market theoretically mean a good time for option selling based strategies in general. If there are further corrections, I expect that to be much less sharp than the one we have already seen and covered call strategies can add some relative outperformance there too (from option premiums earned). The worst time to enter such strategies is when we expect a sharp bull run ahead - that seems ruled out.

The general positive outlook for covered call strategies is only partially applicable for single stock based covered call strategies though. In the case of the YieldMax AMD Option Income Strategy ETF (AMDY), the upcoming rangebound conditions are not necessarily a Strong Buy zone - a rating it had earned a year ago, when AMD's own outlook was bullish after sharp corrections.

For AMDY, we need to move beyond the generic Buy inclination just because of the covered call angle for current market conditions. We need to look at AMD's own thesis too at this point for fresh entries in AMDY. This is because, as we will see in further detail in this analysis, AMDY is not an aggressive covered call writing strategy that maximizes option income alone - it operates using spreads that can capture AMD rallies too. This setup (upside participation over income maximization) makes the strategy more dependent on AMD's own thesis and also benefits less even from a rangebound outlook for AMD.

My current thesis on AMD is bullish in the long term, but does not expect a sharp rally in the near term like we saw in 2025 (from the April lows). That means, the need to capture upside (which is what AMDY is designed for) is limited in the near term. And since income is not prioritized, a flat period for AMD does not necessarily translate to heavy income generation from AMDY. Therefore, I downgrade AMDY from a Strong Buy to a Buy in current conditions. The Strong Buy rating has worked well so far. And a Buy is still supported - only that a far more aggressive option positioning would have been better for income in a flat to slow upside outlook for AMD (near term).

Performance Review and Clues

Investors who were long since my Strong Buy rating in AMDY in April last year would have seen ~90% total returns in what turned out to be an extremely bullish phase for AMD. AMD itself is up by almost 125% since then. The gap in total returns may look significant, but for a strategy that theoretically caps upside (i.e. covered calls alone), this is decent upside capture. Especially when seen in context of the fact that AMD rallied by almost 150% in a matter of months - between mid-April to mid-October. (with many short bursts of concentrated and sharp upside). Covered calls, in theory, can still capture a slower rally where the upside within a given monthly expiry is in the proximity of premiums received from close to ATM strike option selling. But sharp rallies (often 20-30% within a few days and sustained) are tougher to capture for simple covered calls.

That is, unless the strategy is not simply writing calls close to the spot levels. I downloaded the full holdings from the ETF website and saw that as of April 6th 2026, the underlying AMD exposure is 6475 contracts (implemented as a synthetic position of short puts and long calls at the $200 strike). The option income layer is implemented in the form of a laddered call structure across several strikes ranging from $207.5 to $220.

It is difficult to ascertain whether AMDY places strikes aggressively close to the spot prices at the time of setup, although the strikes look close to ATM levels (and some are already ITM). What can be said from the evidence more confidently is the fact that the current call writing in total adds up to 5600 contracts - which is ~87% of the underlying exposure. That is, the option layer does not fully cover the entire portfolio to maximize income. I still reckon that ~87% coverage can lead to decently strong income generation - but there is another structural pointer that explains AMDY's upside over income stance. And that is call spreads - AMDY has 5600 buy call positions at various strikes (not too far away from the written call range) ranging from $217.5 to $227.5. The combination of a staggered option writing layer and a staggered call buy layer means a lot of upside reopens, especially in sharp rallies. The downside to that approach is lower net premiums earned - and we see that in action when we look at the empirical drawdown mitigation data so far.

From late 2024 to the April 2025 lows, when AMD was down by almost half, AMDY had saved around ~7 percentage points of the fall, from premiums earned. That shows the limited drawdown protection you would expect theoretically due to the setup's limitations too - the income is capped at premiums earned and the rest of the drawdown is open by structure. However, for a slower period of downward move over months and for the magnitude of the drop seen, a purer covered call strategy would have earned a bit more.

The above chart still shows some relative performance gains using the AMDY route. The ~15% corrections from November 2025 does not even show any meaningful or sustained performance gains in the AMDY route. That is the call spread mechanism at work - primed for upside capture and deprioritizing income.

Overall, AMDY's structural setup is more aligned to track AMD broadly in a bid to capture upside. The income generation capabilities are not maximized - which means lower income expectations compared to a fully covered and only call selling approach. The trade-off is of course higher upside capture, which works out in terms of total returns over the full cycle, if investors carry a long term bullish thesis in AMD.

The AMD Thesis and Implications

I do have a long term bullish thesis on AMD. I believe AMD will gain on its second source position through a multi-decade AI compute buildout. However, I indicated in my last AMD thesis in November that the long term play might take time to evolve. In the short term, upside appears pulled forward at higher valuations -

AMD's rise since July has been more about valuation rerating than earnings upgrades, so valuations have become more expensive than when I gave it a tactical play tag.

Given that outlook, and further developments seen across the broader AI trade since, where earnings are more likely to grow into valuations, a rangebound outlook in AMD can be formed. Since this is a single stock based covered call strategy, readiness to capture upside is a strength in AMDY, especially over full cycles. In the short term though, it could be a drag. AMDY is not positioned as much for option income as for upside capture.

Given that dual long term and short term forces, the current rating for AMDY is a downgrade to a Buy (from its earlier Strong Buy rating a year ago, which it fully justified in a bull rally for AMD). This downgrade should mean investors are advised to not load up AMDY as much as they did in April last year. But a Buy is still on. For investors who build income exposure systematically across time, this means a reduced check size for AMDY now compared to an all-in investment recommendation earlier.

data.title: WildBrain: Updates Post Deal Close
data.description:
Now that WildBrain's (WILD:CA) (WLDBF) Peanuts sale has closed, I'm revisiting with revised and refined numbers. This is a catalyst-rich story with incredibly compelling valuation.

As a reminder, WILD owns the intellectual property for kids programming (Strawberry Shortcake, Teletubbies, etc) a children's content studio, a consumer licensing business (mainly for kids IP) and a digital distribution business. The company used to own 41% of the Peanuts (Charlie Brown/Snoopy) franchise. They just sold that to Sony for $630 million, which paid down all debt and left them with $40 million cash, in addition to the $69 million of cash already on the balance sheet. All numbers in this write-up are Canadian.

I'll walk through the different divisions separately before I get to catalysts and a SOTP (Sum of the Parts)

The Studio

The writer strike cost the studio about 20mm of EBITDA. Business is ramping back up post the strike and over 75% of capacity is sold out over the medium term. Reinvestment into AI/automation should result in further upside. I could that business getting to 40-50mm of EBITDA. For purposes of this write-up, I'm going to use $18 million of EBITDA with quick upside to $30 million.

Digital distribution

Digital Distribution is mostly through fast channel (Free Ad-Supported Streaming, examples are Pluto TV and Tubi) and Spark, the company's own FAST channel. Right now, the company distributes mostly its own content through these channel and generates mid-single digit EBITDA but grows revenue double digits.

Intellectual Property

Post the Peanuts sales. Strawberry Shortcake is the big brand, with Teletubbies just behind. The company also owns franchises like Caillou and Inspector Gadget. The company gets paid 10-15% royalties on wholesale revenues, which are typically 50% of retail sales. This revenue is over 80% margin once a company is past the J-Curve (upfront investment in the brand). Strawberry Shortcake is in the J-Curve right now as the company rebuilds the franchise after years of neglect under ownership by American Greetings and Iconix Brand Group before WildBrain bought it along with the Peanuts rights in 2017.

For context, Iconix paid USD $105 million for Strawberry Shortcake in 2015. They sold it and the 80% Peanuts not owned by Charles Schulz's family to WildBrain in 2017 for USD $345 million. WildBrain in turn sold 49% of that 80% Peanuts stake to Sony in 2018 for C$235 million (~USD $180 million). Compared to the recent C$630 million for the rest of Peanuts, that initial Peanuts sale looks terrible, but Peanuts was a much smaller franchise. Also, considering the 49% Peanuts sales recouped over half of the entire purchase back from Iconix back in about a year, one can imply that they got Strawberry Shortcake for free.

Exact data is not great, but Strawberry Shortcake had been about a $500 million annual retail sales property in the mid-2000s. That implies $250 million of wholesale revenues and $25mm of royalties. WildBrain had dedicated most of its resources to building the Peanuts brand in recent years and started investing in Strawberry Shortcake about two years ago. That investment is already bearing fruit. In fiscal '24, Shortcake did about $67mm of retail sales with about $0 EBITDA margin. For fiscal '25, that number grew over 200% to $200 million. The November earnings call Q1 of fiscal '26 included the update, "Watch time on YouTube was up 20% and average view duration was up nearly 300% for Strawberry Shortcake in the quarter, and we continue to see social media engagement rising. Consumer demand for Strawberry merchandise continued to grow, with consumer products revenue for Strawberry up 115% year-over-year, with healthy diversification across licensees." If we apply just 100% growth to Strawberry for the full year of fiscal '26, it implies $400 million of retail sales, $200 million of wholesale sales and $20-30 million of EBITDA. If the retail growth rate halved again for '27, EBITDA could grow to around $45 million.

I believe Teletubbies (acquired in 2013) is about one year behind Strawberry. At $100 million of retail sales and negligible EBITDA for fiscal '25 growing to $150 retail sales and low to mid-single digit EBITDA for fiscal '26 growing to mid-teens EBITDA in '27.

Consumer Property Licensing

CPLG is a nice business. It sells licensing rights for the company's brands and on behalf of third parties. They just signed a multi-year agreement with Sony as part of the Peanuts sale. Given Peanuts alone, I can see CPLG getting to $10mm+ of EBITDA. These sales groups are specialized and hard to recreate.

Corporate Overhead

Following the sale, the company is cutting a lot of overhead. I believe it will settle at $20 million for '27.

Valuation

Sony just paid about 23x EBITDA for the remaining Peanuts stake, close to the average multiple for children's IP over 35 years. I'll apply discounts to Strawberry Shortcake and Teletubbies to be conservative, despite their growth potential.

On CPLG it's a nice strategic asset, with very high cash conversion, and I believe there are several strategic acquirers. Despite that, I still take a discount to what I think a strategic would pay.

The company actually ran a process to sell the studio before the writer's strike. They got 11-14x EBITDA bids, consistent with production studio multiples. 9-story, which operated at only 33% capacity and did mostly 3rd Party, sold for 10x last year. Still, I will match that multiple.

Digital Distribution is growing a ton and is where the puck is going. I have no comps, but I feel like 10x is a conservative valuation.

Given the potential growth, I'll give ranges for what I think the SOTP could look like (depending on whether buyers use current versus potential cash flows). I give a range based on FY '26 and FY '27 EBITDA numbers and in Canadian Dollars. I believe I'm conservative on EBITDA levels and multiples.

Asset FY '26 EBITDA FY '27 EBITDA Value Multiple
Production Studio $18mm $30mm $180-300mm 10x
Digital Distribution $8mm $20mm $80-200mm 10x
Consumer Licensing $8mm $15mm $72-135mm 9x
Strawberry Shortcake $25mm $30mm $375-450mm 15x
Teletubbies $8mm $15mm $80-150mm 10x
Corporate Overhead $20mm $20mm -$200mm 10x
Total $47mm $90mm $578mm-1.15 billion

These numbers compare extremely well to the post-Peanuts sales valuation

Market Cap (@ $1.29) $275 million
Cash $119 million
Enterprise Value $156 million
EV/'26 EBITDA ($47 million) 3.3x
EV/'27 EBITDA ($90 million) 1.7x

Catalyst

The company has stated that they are looking at share repurchases with the $119 million of cash (albeit some is restricted) and are willing to leverage 2x. At $47 million of EBITDA, and if we just use the $40 million net cash from the Peanuts sale, that gives them almost $140 million of dry powder for purchases. Canada has two mechanisms for share repurchases SIB (Substantial Issuer Bid), which is like a tender and NCIB (Normal Course Issuer Bid). There are different rules for each. For an NCIB, the company may repurchase:

  • Over any 12-month period: The greater of 5% of the issued and outstanding shares of that class or 10% of the public float (as of the first day of the period).
  • In any 30-day period: Generally no more than 2% of the issued and outstanding shares (this is the "normal course" volume limit to avoid market impact).

For an SIB, the company may offer to purchase any percentage of its outstanding shares (including 100% of a class, subject to practical and legal constraints). The main requirements are:

  • A formal bid circular with detailed disclosure.
  • Minimum deposit period (35 days for issuer bids).
  • Proportionate take-up if the bid is oversubscribed (unless it's for all shares or structured differently).
  • Equal treatment of shareholders.
  • Regulatory filing and, in many cases, stock exchange acceptance.

I believe the company will try a combination of the two. I could see an SIB for 40 to 80 million shares and an NCIB for the balance of the repurchase firepower.

While this might not seem like a big deal, one firm, Fine Capital, owns just under 45% of the stock. They have been involved for years, and I can't fathom that they would sell at anywhere near these levels. That leaves around 120 million shares outstanding. If the company tried to buy north of 40 million shares, it would reduce the free float considerably.

With the Peanuts deal closed in early March, I believe we will see some sort of repurchase news shortly.

Risks

The main risks here are another writers strike and a major fumble growing the IP. Management could also surprise by doing an acquisition rather than repurchasing shares, but I think that's highly unlikely.

Conclusion

Post the Peanuts sale, this company is massively derisked. The sale also proves the value of the embedded IP. I could see this name more than doubling in short order, driven by growth, operational improvements and repurchases.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Compliance Crisis Raises Sell Question
data.description:
Super Micro Computer (SMCI) has fallen 33.6% since my last report after allegations emerged that Super Micro Computer employees have been involved in a scheme to circumvent export controls. While Super Micro Computer is not a named defendant in the case, it is a bad look for a company that already did not have a great look. In this report, I discuss whether there is any angle in which a buy rating can be maintained or whether it is time to sell. I do note that any buy angle would be a higher risk.

The Facts On The Smuggling Case

The facts as we know them today are that three individuals, including the co-founder of Super Micro Computer, a contractor, and a sales manager in the Taiwan office, have been charged with alleged involvement in a scheme to illegally ship Nvidia-powered GPU services to China, which fall under strict export controls. This has led to around $2.5 billion in sales generated since 2024. While Super Micro Computer is not a defendant in the case, it is definitely not a good look for the company.

Another Black Eye For Super Micro Computer

While the smuggling case is already a bad thing, it is definitely not the first time that Super Micro Computer has been in a negative spotlight. A filing from the SEC described that the company prematurely recognized revenues while understating expenses between 2015 and 2017. The case was settled with a $17.5 million penalty and a cease-and-desist order, which simply means the company must not violate securities laws again.

In 2024, EY stated that it was no longer willing to be associated with the financial statements of the company. That type of auditor friction is rare, and the recent smuggling case may bring this auditor friction back into focus.

The prior settlement does not legally amplify export-related penalties, but it raises the stakes on perception and credibility. If it is found that Super Micro Computer’s compliance control has been insufficient, then there is a narrative shift from isolated past issues to recurring governance risk due to inadequate compliance control. I am no lawyer by any means, but in my field, which is engineering compliance, compliance control and business ethics are critical. With my experience in engineering, I can only say that what we see at Super Micro Computer looks extremely bad, as it looks more like a pattern of violations of various laws rather than isolated events. How Super Micro Computer is going to distance itself from the case or participate in the case and what the Department of Justice may bring forward is going to be critical for the company.

Concerns About Management Guidance Existed Before

In my opinion, apart from any financial damages that this may bring, either through potential settlements, increased compliance control costs, licenses being revoked, or a combination of the aforementioned, there is a big credibility issue for management right now. That becomes particularly problematic for their financial guidance. You can really wonder what that guidance is worth.

In my prior report, before the scandal became apparent, I already made the following two comments on the company’s guidance being too upbeat:

Comment 1

The obvious risk to this guidance is that the company may once again miss sales. SMCI management has simply not been shown to have the best track record in estimating its quarterly sales.

Comment 2

…there is some uncertainty on whether SMCI will be able to deliver on its guidance. If it doesn't, I would see it as a major indication that the company is unable to accurately assess external supply chain challenges and shifts in customer requirements and the impact that may have on the company's earnings. If they are able to deliver on the guidance, we would be able to see how the company's ramp-up is coming along. However, what stands is that revenues are not limited by demand but rather by overall supply chain health and readiness.

While we lack insights into the exact quarters in which the scheme allegedly ran, what I mostly found is that sales were concentrated in 2024 until May 2025. Over that time, the company actually missed estimates by $345.6 million, and if we take FY24 until the end of FY25, we get $123 million in revenue beats. What this really points at is that the illegal sales were more than a structural part of the revenue expectations from analysts, which is not surprising given that they are often based on management outlooks, but depending on the timeframe, one could say that 6.8% to 8% of the business’ revenues can be tied to export control violations. That is significant.

A Look At Estimates For Super Micro Computer

One of the most interesting parts is that estimates for Super Micro Computer have barely changed. In fact, since my last report, analysts now estimate another 10-14% growth. EBITDA estimates for FY26 have increased by 10%, while they are 0%-3% lower in the years after.

The Precedent On Export Control Violations

Whether Super Micro Computer will face any financial backlash beyond loss of sales to China has yet to be determined. It is, however, useful to look at what the law states and what we have seen in precedent cases. Violations of export controls can be met with a penalty of $374,474 per violation or twice the value of the transaction, whichever is greater, according to the Bureau of Industry and Security:

As of January 15, 2025, the maximum administrative monetary penalty is $374,474 per violation or twice the value of the transaction, whichever is greater. This amount is adjusted annually for inflation.

In my opinion, any potential settlement for Super Micro Computer could mean a $5 billion headwind.

However, typically penalties and settlements have been much lower. In 2023, Seagate Technology was paid a $300 million penalty for violating export regulations on a transaction value of $1.1 billion, or 27.3% of the transaction value. ZTE paid a $1.19 billion penalty for shipping telecommunication equipment to Iran and North Korea, but a transaction value remains unknown, while Keysight Technologies paid $6.6 million on an unquantified transaction value.

I am currently reserving 40% of the value, or $1 billion, for any potential settlements. It usually takes several years for a settlement to be reached and executed, so the timing of any potential settlement could shift between years.

Super Micro Computer Stock Could Be A Very High-Risk Buy

For Super Micro Computer, I have implemented the numbers as shown above. The adjustments to estimates are shown below:

  • Around 8% of sales have been removed from future revenue outlooks, consistent with the 6%-8% share that the illegal sales previously had.
  • Margins that these sales had have been subtracted from EBITDA in 2026.
  • A $1 billion reduction in EBITDA in FY26 to reflect any potential settlement.
  • A reduction of the free cash flow in FY27 reflecting a potential settlement in FY26 (one-year lag in payment) and the cash reductions in FY26-FY28.
  • A loss of incremental margin from the 8% of sales adjustment.

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I am adjusting my price target to $36.04, which indicates 55% upside. The buy rating remains, but it is one that is contingent on my view that executives at Super Micro Computer must depart as part of the recovery of the company. In my view, CEO Liang has faced one too many scandals at Super Micro Computer, and it gives the impression that he is not in control of the situation and, frankly, has little affinity with strict compliance control mechanisms. For that reason, I can also understand that an investor would strongly disagree with a buy rating.

A visualization of the key metrics shows a significant decline in EBITDA margins due to the adjustments. In the years ahead, margins are expected to recover, but we also note that they will remain substantially below previously seen levels, which also may cast some doubt on whether Super Micro Computer actually has a product that the market is willing to pay for. In some way, with higher GPU costs, it looks a lot as if Super Micro Computer's model is more one that creates markup margins rather than a percentage on costs being added. So, as input costs rise and the margins are more like a flat fee, the margins expressed in a percentage drop.

So, that is another warning signal. Free cash flow is expected to be negative this year and into the next year. Net debt leverage is set to pop due to the sudden fall in EBITDA but recover in the years after. In some way, Super Micro Computer is a good example of how one mistake out of a series of mistakes destroys value for years to come.

Conclusion: Super Micro Computer Has A Credibility Issue

Super Micro Computer still has growth prospects, but it is clear that something needs to change in their legal and financial compliance because the company has slipped too many times on that already. I am a strong believer that change is cultivated from the top-down and not bottom-up, and that means that for Super Micro Computer stock to be a buy again, it needs to happen hand-in-hand with changes at the top. That is almost a requisite for the company to recover credibility with investors, but also with customers, suppliers, and other stakeholders. With a founding member allegedly being part of the scheme, the top-down cultivation of business ethics becomes even more pressing.

I believe that if you weigh the ethical and compliance angle of this scandal and the company's history, there is a sell angle, but if you look at fundamentals, there could be a buy angle. Do you weigh governance risks heavier than fundamentals here, or do you see this as a high-risk opportunity? I’d be interested to hear your view in the comment section.

data.title: Stoneridge Still Offers Excellent Growth Potential
data.description:
The last few months have just been brutal for shareholders of Stoneridge (SRI). Back in December of last year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a "Buy" candidate. This was even though financial performance leading up to that point had been disappointing. However, I was optimistic about the future outlook for the company, especially because of growth associated with its MirrorEye product line. Since then, however, things have worsened further.

Revenue, profits, and cash flows have taken a hit. However, management seems optimistic about the long-term outlook even as 2026 is slated to be a down year for the firm. When you consider how cheap shares are, it's difficult to imagine a scenario where a further decline is warranted. Even if financial performance stays around where it is right now, I would see a flatline in terms of performance at worst. And if management can come through on the promises that they have made for the next couple of years, I would argue that the company could see tremendous appreciation. Because of this favorable risk-to-reward scenario, I believe that maintaining it as a "Buy" makes sense, even though the stock is down 17.1% since my previous article about it.

Shares are still worth taking for a drive

At first glance, you might think that I am unreasonable for remaining bullish about Stoneridge in light of recent fundamental performance. Take data from the most recent quarter as an example. That happens to be the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Revenue for the business during that window of time amounted to $205.2 million. That represented a drop of 6% compared to the $218.3 million that the company reported a year earlier. The decline in revenue was not the only pain that we saw. Bottom-line results worsened also. The business went from a net loss of $6.1 million to a net loss of $76.9 million. Most of that decline, however, is the result of impairment charges and a surge in income taxes. Actual adjusted net profits fared far better, declining only from negative $5 million to negative $14.7 million. Operating cash flow contracted from $15.8 million to $12.5 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we get a drop from $4.1 million to negative $2.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company was cut by nearly half, from $6 million to $3.4 million.

The chart above shows financial results for the 2025 fiscal year compared to 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, worsened without exception. This is disappointing to say the least. But in all honesty, it's not terribly surprising. To really understand what is going on here, though, we need to dig under the hood. And the best way to do this would be to look at each of its operating segments in turn. The largest of these is none other than its Electronics segment, which is responsible for the advanced driver information solutions, vision systems, connectivity and compliance solutions, and control modules, that the company produces. Most notably, this is the part of the company responsible for its MirrorEye product, which is aimed at eliminating blind spots for drivers.

Management has been touting MirrorEye as a huge opportunity for the business. And yet, even with that being the case, revenue declined year over year for the final quarter from $149.3 million to $133.2 million. This decline in sales was, according to the company, because of lower customer production volumes in North America and Europe for the commercial vehicle end markets. Commercial vehicle is the key term here. After all, the consumer side of the vehicle space actually had a really good year in 2026 because of customers front-loading demand out of anticipation of tariff-related cost increases and the expiration of the electric vehicle tax credit in September of last year. But that is not the same thing as the commercial side of the business, which has been severely negatively affected by broader economic conditions. After the COVID-19 pandemic, there was a surge in activity here. But that led to overcapacity and, when combined with worsening economic conditions, has put a lot of pain onto the industry. So to see weakness in this market is not shocking to me in the least bit.

Overall profitability for this part of the company also worsened, dropping from $5.2 million to just $0.2 million. And for the year as a whole, the decline in revenue from $566 million to $526.4 million caused profits to plummet from $25.6 million to $14.3 million. This does not imply that everything was bad. According to management, MirrorEye revenue growth was 69% year over year. That took overall sales for this product line up to $111 million. And when it comes to the OEM side of things, revenue growth was an astounding 84%. And in fact, during 2025, the company announced new business awards amounting to $830 million. That's inclusive of the largest award in the company's history.

The good news was that this was the only area of weakness for the company from a sales standpoint. The Control Devices segment for the company, as an example, saw revenue grow from $63.2 million to $64.4 million. That 2% increase was thanks to higher passenger vehicle sales in North America, like I mentioned. China also contributed to some of this expansion. Unfortunately, segment profits did worsen from negative $1.8 million to negative $22.8 million. But on an adjusted basis, the segment saw a 20 basis point improvement because of lower selling, general, administrative, and engineering costs. Finally, the Stoneridge Brazil segment saw revenue grow nicely from $12.4 million to $16.6 million, driving segment profits up from $0.1 million to $1.4 million. It appears as though the company benefited from higher OEM and aftermarket sales in that country. A favorable foreign currency change also helped the bottom line here.

As disappointing as the final quarter of 2025 was, there are reasons to be optimistic for the business. This might not seem to be the case when you first look at it. After all, when it comes to 2026, management expects revenue to fall from $861.3 million last year to between $625 million and $650 million this year. However, this is comparing apples to oranges. I say this because, according to the company, these new sales figures for the business exclude its Control Devices segment, which management sold at the end of January of this year in a deal valued at $59 million. When we strip this out from the equation, revenue for the company last year would have been $611.5 million. So actually, what we are getting is a rise in sales.

Most of this should come from incremental MirrorEye revenue of around $50 million. That should take sales for the business up to more than $160 million. This includes a significant take rate of between 35% and 50% in Europe compared to just 5% to 15% here in the United States. As revenue increases on an organic basis, profitability should improve as well. The current expectation that the company has is for EBITDA of between $20 million and $25 million. That is down from the $25 million reported for last year. But again, when we make the adjustments necessary for the change in business structure, EBITDA in 2025 would have been $14.2 million.

Beyond 2026, management expects even more growth. Some of this will come from OEM end market growth that falls outside of MirrorEye. However, the company expects sales of MirrorEye to expand by $35 million in 2027. Further expansion here and elsewhere for the company should push revenue up to between $850 million and $1 billion by the year 2030. From that, we are looking at annual EBITDA of $80 million at the low end and $120 million at the high end.

In all honesty, even if the business falls partially short of these expectations, I think that there are reasons to be optimistic. In the chart above, you can see projected multiples based on what management has pledged for the next few years. There could be some uncertainty here because of changes in interest rates thanks to the cash proceeds that the company received associated with its asset sale that could push net debt down. So I do think that relying on the EV-to-EBITDA approach to valuing the company makes more sense here. But regardless, especially on a forward basis for the next couple of years, shares look quite appealing.

In the chart above, you can see how five other similar firms are priced. I then took average data from these five businesses and calculated, in the chart below, how much upside Stoneridge would experience if it were to trade at those multiples while achieving their targets for 2027 and 2030. The chart also shows how much upside the stock would experience if it were to trade at the multiples it achieved using 2025 performance. What we have here is a business that would justify upside of anywhere from 76% to 252.8% when it comes to 2027, and upside of between 300% and 701.8% when looking forward into 2030. This is significant, especially the 2030 figures. Even capturing half of the low end of the 2030 figure would mean an upside of 20.1% annually. That is well above what the market can be expected to get of between 10% and 11% per annum. And at the high end of the range, we would be looking at an annualized upside of 51.6%.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that is available right now, I maintain that Stoneridge is an attractive prospect, even if it is currently on a bumpy road. Because of broader economic uncertainty, specifically when it comes to the logistics space, I am not ready to upgrade it further at this time. But I am considering doing that, especially if I see the share price drop much further from where it has. For now, however, I will keep it rated a solid "Buy."

data.title: Iran War Risk Appears To Be In Danger Zone
data.description:
By James Picerno

The war with Iran continues to roil financial markets. At some point the risk will peak, providing context for deciding if the worst has passed. Estimating that point will be useful but also challenging in real time, in part because it’s a task that requires analyzing a wide variety of data. As a first approximation, TMC Research is tracking an aggregate of five relevant risk dimensions based on market signals via a set of ETFs.

There are several ways to estimate war-related risk through a markets-based lens. TMC Research’s Iran War Risk Index (IWRI) represents an initial estimate of war-related perceptions. This model can easily be updated in real time for tracking how markets are reacting to news events. Short-term changes can be noisy, and so, the daily data are smoothed with a LOESS regression model to highlight the trend.

IWRI is comprised of five inputs that are germane for monitoring the war. ETFs are used as proxies for each variable:

• Crude oil (USO)
• US dollar (UUP)
• Stock market volatility (VIXY)
• Credit spread (IEI-HYG spread)
• Inflation risk sentiment (TIP-IEF spread)

The weights of the five factors are slightly skewed relative to an equal weighting and give oil the highest weight (30%), followed by 25% for the credit spread, 20% for the US dollar, 15% for stock market volatility, and 10% for inflation risk.

The current reading continues to show an elevated level of risk, as of April 6. The data are shown in z-scores, and so, levels above +1 equate with high risk. Accordingly, a drop from the current level below +1.0 may be an early clue that markets are starting to price in de-escalation. Such a change in sentiment, for now, doesn’t look imminent, with IWRI trading above +2.0, which is far above the pre-war range.

A sustained decline in IWRI toward its pre-war range would be a sign that markets see the danger fading. Until then, elevated readings suggest that a high-risk narrative remains in play.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: SoundHound AI: Path To Profitability (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
SoundHound AI (SOUN) is a software leader in AI-driven voice technology and is therefore benefiting massively from the expansion of the agentic AI market. The start-up achieved a major inflection point in the fourth-quarter as it reported a significant turnaround in its net profit situation and it was highly successful in signing up new customers in its. In addition, Soundhound AI expanded its footprint and managed to diversify its customer base, by adding new paying clients in healthcare and financial services.

While SoundHound AI is experiencing a revenue surge, and positive net profits, shares of the software company have recently revalued lower amid growing concerns about the disruptive impact of AI on SaaS companies. I believe concerns are exaggerated as the utilization of artificial intelligence is actually a catalyst that helps SoundHound AI develop its product portfolio and grow its market presence. With shares recently dropping below the 3-year average P/S ratio, I believe the investment backdrop and recent accomplishments justify a rating up-grade.

Previous rating

I up-graded shares of SoundHound AI to buy in my last work in November -- Strong Agentic AI Growth -- after the AI voice technology enterprise saw positive traction with its Amelia 7 voice technology platform. Further, in response to acquisition success, Soundhound AI is seeing significant growth in its top line, while also publishing an improvement in its profitability profile. I like the contrarian investment setup here in the sense that recent SaaS concerns have heavily weighed on the software sector. With SoundHound AI continuing to deliver strong results, I believe the software AI company could be set for a revaluation to the upside in 2026.

A key player in voice AI technology

Soundhound AI has achieved considerable sign-up momentum for new clients and as a result posted record 2025 revenues of $168.9M, showing a nearly-100% year-over-year increase. This revenue growth is chiefly driven by an expansion of the start-up’s customer base, especially as the adoption of AI agents is now widely accepted in the enterprise market. In the fourth-quarter alone, SoundHound signed over 100 new customer deals, including major agreements with a Japanese automotive OEM, a top U.S. telecom provider as well as global restaurant brands like Panda Express and Jersey Mike's... all of which are using the firm's AI-powered voice technology platform to streamline customer service and improve engagement.

Because of this significant customer acquisition momentum, SoundHound AI was able to grow its revenues to $55.1M in the fourth-quarter, showing 49% year-over-year growth. Most importantly, SoundHound AI achieved a major inflection point in terms of profitability as well, though mainly because of a change in the fair value of contingent acquisition liabilities (totaling $84.6M). As a result, the start-up was able to drastically improve its net profit situation for the fourth-quarter: the AI voice technology platform published a net profit of $40.1M in the fourth-quarter which marked its first quarterly profit on a GAAP basis since Q1'25.

SoundHound AI is seeing surging gross profits as well and, on the back of new strategic partnerships with companies such as Red Lobster, an improving in its gross profit margin. In the most recent quarter, Soundhound AI posted a gross profit margin, on a GAAP level, of ~39%. With new partnerships and at-scale product deliveries in the financial services and healthcare sectors, I see a catalyst for higher gross margins for SoundHound AI in 2026.

Soundhound AI’s valuation

The ‘SaaSpocalypse’ had a massive impact on SoundHound AI this year insofar as the firm's valuation multiplier was concerned. The SaaSpocalypse encapsulates investors’ fears that the utilization of artificial intelligence may negatively impact the business models of software companies. As a result, valuation factors for AI-driven software (and hardware) companies have reset lower in 2026 which I believe is making SoundHound AI now a much better deal for investors.

Currently, shares of Soundhound AI are trading at a forward price-to-revenue ratio of 10.0X. While shares of the AI voice platform are still expensive, they are also trading significantly below the firm’s longer term, 3-year average P/S ratio of 14.0X, potentially signaling undervaluation. High-growth AI-powered software platform Applovin (APP), which is also expected to grow at least 25% annually over the next three years, is trading at a forward price-to-revenue ratio of 14.4X.

Most importantly, sentiment towards AI software stocks has taken a serious hit lately, which to me makes SoundHound AI even more interesting as a growth play on AI technology in the agentic AI market. Given that the agentic AI market is expanding rapidly (see my last work on SOUN), I believe sooner rather than later the software tech market is going to go through a major repricing to the upside again. As a result, I would not be surprised to see the software enterprise trade back up to a ~14X P/S ratio... which would be in-line with its historical P/S ratio as well as the valuation multiplier of Applovin. This would calculate to a fair value estimate for SoundHound AI of $9.40 per-share, implying 40% upside.

Primary risks

The main risk that I see for the start-up relates to slowing top line growth in 2026: while the AI voice technology company is still growing rapidly, its growth its maturing and the firm's current revenue guidance for FY 2026 ($225-260M) implies 33-54% year-over-year growth. This guidance implies a reduction from the firm's posted 99% growth rate achieved in the 2025 fiscal year. As investors' focus is set to shift towards profitability, however, I believe slowing growth is already priced into SoundHound AI's shares. What would get me to review the company (and my strong buy rating) is if SoundHound AI were to see a reversal in its gross profit margin trajectory or if it lost customers in its core AI service business.

Final thoughts

SoundHound AI is growing its core business and expanding its reach into other sectors such as healthcare and financial services. As a result, Soundhound AI is seeing considerable traction in gaining new customers which has led to a nearly 100% increase in revenues in the 2025 fiscal year. The voice AI technology company achieved a major inflection point in the fourth-quarter as well as its published positive net income. The gross profit margin trend is encouraging, and so are recent customer wins.

SoundHound AI's growth proposition is very strong, in my opinion, despite an expected growth deceleration in FY 2026, but the improving profitability profile and up-trending gross profit margins overall indicate a healthy investment proposition. I also believe the recent repricing to the downside amid exaggerated SaaS fears creates an engagement opportunity for long term investors and I am up-grading shares to strong buy.

data.title: Beyond The Deadline: What Markets Are Still Not Pricing In
data.description:
We're entering a new month carrying the same war. The past seven days have brought more painfully mixed signals from the Trump administration on when the war will end. Increasingly, this war has become about the Strait of Hormuz and when it’ll open. We saw some relief this Monday morning, in hopes of that happening sooner than expected. An oil tanker carrying Iraqi crude passed through the Strait this Sunday after Iran exempted it from transit restrictions. The oil on its back is headed to Malaysia, making it one of the seven vessels cleared for transit. Iran has approved transit for vessels without U.S. or Israeli ties.

The Iraqi tanker is not the only one to make it through; according to Reuters’ Sunday report, over the past couple of days, “three Omani-operated tankers, a French-owned container ship and a Japanese-owned gas carrier have crossed the strait.” Control of the Strait is the real leverage, at least for now, and President Trump’s posts over the long weekend confirm, in our opinion, that very fact.

We’ve seen the deadline for the president’s ultimatum for Iran to either open the Strait or see its infrastructure "obliterated" extended more times than inspires confidence. Part of why the extensions look odd is that the delays are supposedly based on progress in negotiations, even as Iranian officials deny any talks. Markets were moving in line with Trump’s Truth Social posts for most of March, but what is becoming more apparent is that the positive momentum in response to each post is losing steam compared to the earlier days of the war. The market is waking up to the fact that this is not a year-ago April, meaning this is not an internal affair that Trump can fix independently; instead, there is an entirely other entity involved, and it takes two to tango, especially in matters of war and peace.

The latest deadline set forth is for Iran to open the Strait by 8:00 PM ET on Tuesday, April 7th, or face the consequences. Iran has already rejected the deadline and also rejected ceasfire proposal, stating that they want a permanent end to the war.

While oil traded lower on Monday amid hopes of vessels passing through, no two days seem to carry the same narrative in this market, especially not with Tuesday's looming deadline. Even with signs of relief, there is a widening disconnect between the paper and the physical market that is seemingly overlooked. The price of real-world oil cargo transactions has hit an 18-year high while Brent crude futures still trade below 2022 levels. Mike Wirth, CEO of Chevron, confirmed this gap, warning:

There are very real, physical manifestations of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz that are working their way around the world and through the system that I don’t think are fully priced into the futures curves on oil.

This is a big problem, and there isn’t really any other way to put it. It means that companies buying oil right now are paying more than the forward-looking market prices. These companies’ input costs are going to shoot up dramatically higher than what markets are bracing for, impacting margins. It simultaneously means the policy responses are potentially being calibrated to the wrong number.

Looking at the glass half full, this discrepancy suggests that financial markets, at least for now, aren’t pricing in a full panic. There is still faith in Trump’s art of the deal. We reaffirm our view that Trump wants an exit from the current situation, as we discussed last Monday. We think Iran also wants to end the conflict, as evidenced by the current government’s own claims. From this perspective, we’re looking at short-term volatility on negotiations but see a resolution during Q2.

The tech supply chain

The S&P 500 (SP500) has closed the month of April higher “80% of the time over the past 20 years.” Q1 was disastrous with the S&P 500 down 4.6%, and all eyes are on April for salvation (again, not divorced from the crash and rally we saw last year around this time). So far, the month hasn’t gone badly, with the index up modestly. The oil disruption remains underway, although some vessels are passing through. It’s not clear whether this is a sustainable flow, and we’re still seeing Asia, the hub of chip-making, facing the largest backlash from the current shortage, given that +80% of the Middle East's oil flows to Asia.

The real overarching concern is that even if we get a resolution to the war in Q2, we’re facing a relief rally that’ll be kept in check by higher costs in the short term, driving inventory hoarding amid supply concerns while end demand lags. The classic risk that emerges from this is an imbalance in demand-supply dynamics, creating a need for a potential inventory correction. This would still be the case even if the war ends tomorrow, because it’ll take time to get Middle East production back to pre-war levels, and production won’t recover evenly across the board. The International Energy Agency estimates that +40 energy assets have been “severely or very severely” damaged by the war. It’ll be easier to revive production levels in areas where production was reduced rather than halted. For Iraq to restore its crude oil exports to 3.4M barrels/day, for example, it’ll take a week. Meanwhile, for Qatar’s Ras Laffan, it could take as much as five years to repair the damage.

Jim Krane, Rice University’s Baker Institute fellow, noted that:

You can’t just push the pause button when oil flows are inconvenient…Interrupting such huge petroleum supply chains causes cascading effects across the globe.

We think investors should keep in mind that the supply chain will need time, even after the war’s resolution, to recalibrate itself. We see potential play in the analog peer group amid the current imbalanced demand-supply dynamic. We first discussed this in last week’s coverage, noting that “when macro uncertainty looms, analogs tend to see short-term benefit from pull-ins and double ordering.” We’re seeing confirmation data points for this thesis, with analog companies raising prices despite still-soft end-demand. Texas Instruments (TXN) is reported by Digitimes to be raising prices starting this month by as much as 85% of some products, following other analog peers like Analog Devices, Onsemi, Infineon, and NXP that’ve raised prices in Q1. Digitimes notes in the report from mid-March that:

“The current round of price increases is not limited to TI. Several European and US semiconductor manufacturers with less bargaining power than TI issued price hike notices earlier.”

A report from the South China Morning Post adds that Chinese analog chipmakers are also announcing price increases in line with the international peer group, including domestic players: Novosense Microelectronics, SG Micro, Fortior Technology, Halo Microelectronics, Silan Micro, and Kiwi Instruments. While the higher pricing is in part driven by increased power demand for AI build-out, it alone can’t explain the across-the-board increase. We see analog as an attractive area in the current environment. It’s also worth noting that this peer group has been more resilient than the bunch this year, as shown in the YTD chart below.

The broader price uplift we’re seeing, specifically from memory, continues to weigh on end demand in PC and smartphones. On the latter, memory now makes up +50% of the total build price, up from 18%. Meanwhile, on the smartphone front, MediaTek and Qualcomm are reportedly cutting smartphone AP orders at TSMC (TSM), citing “estimated cuts of 10-15% in wafer starts on 4/3nm processes.” Softer PC and smartphone TAM will also be a big theme for the current quarter, particularly given the overall squeezed-spending backdrop, with higher prices at the pump and sticker inflation amid higher costs.

Jim Kelleher, Argus Research’s director of research, argues that consumers will be prioritizing essentials over tech spending, stating that “You can't skimp on gas…You've got to get to work.” JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon also flagged concerns over oil and commodity price shocks that'll "lead to stickier inflation and ultimately higher interest rates than markets currently expect." This is the ripple effect of the current war that’ll likely outlast the war itself in the near term.

U.S.-China Chip Wars are not on the back burner

While Iran is taking headlines, the U.S.-China chip war is far from forgotten, specifically in light of the Super Micro Computer (SMCI) issues last month. Tuesday morning, Dutch ASML (ASML) is falling sharply following the proposal of U.S. legislation to further restrict DUV exports to China. The proposal, called the MATCH ACT (Multilateral Alignment of Technology Controls on Hardware), was announced earlier this month and aims to prevent Chinese domestic players from buying chip-making tools and to chokehold China's technological advancements, specifically targeting players like SMIC, Huawei, Hua Hong, YMTC, and others.

The draft legislation would essentially push allies of the U.S. to follow a "deny by default" export restriction and, in doing so, extend the U.S.'s export controls onto foreign-made tools. ASML’s more advanced EUV tools have never been approved for sale to China, but its less-advanced DUV tools have been approved and enjoyed strong demand, with China accounting for as much as 42% of ASML’s quarterly sales in 3Q25 and 36% in Q4 '25. ASML expects China sales to normalize this year to the 20% level. In our opinion, ASML's longer-term upside comes from EUV demand, which is expanding as the industry transitions from memory to logic.

The other side of this conversation is about what China could do in retaliation. The fear here mainly centers on a potential invasion of Taiwan, home to TSMC, which would drive the entire tech sector into a wall. We don't expect China to act on this front, as it would undermine its own technological ambitions, given that ASML can remotely shut down its advanced tools in the event of an invasion and that China is still a customer, accounting for ~9% of TSMC's total sales. The MATCH ACT headline shows that the chip wars remain an important part of the conversation and could be among the issues addressed during President Trump’s visit to Beijing in mid-May.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Launching Main Street Alpha: Focusing On Long-Term Wealth Creation
data.description:
Introduction

Today, I'm extremely excited to launch Main Street Alpha. This new investing service on Seeking Alpha was created to help investors focus on long-term wealth creation through macro-driven research, structural market trends, and high-conviction investment ideas.

Main Street Alpha combines deep macroeconomic analysis, geopolitical insights, and company-level research to identify the opportunities that benefit from long-term trends instead of short-term market noise. Members receive exclusive research reports, high-conviction investment ideas, three model portfolios, direct access to me, and a private member chat where members discuss the markets and long-term investment strategies.

For a limited time, the first group of members for Main Street Alpha can sign up for the service at the price of $499. The price increases to $599 after this special offer expires.

Main Street Alpha represents the next stage of the journey my followers and I have been on together for more than 10 years on Seeking Alpha.

Introducing Main Street Alpha

I started investing at the age of 15. That was 15 years ago. As some of you may know (because I have brought it up so much in my research), what I did back then was flat-out gambling. I still remember how I felt when I nearly blew up my account on Christmas Eve and had to pretend everything was fine during the holiday.

This is obviously an incredibly silly story, yet in hindsight, all of these mistakes were terrific building blocks for what turned out to be a career in finance and publication. Moreover, because I experimented with almost every type of investing and trading style/strategy, I was able to early on figure out the best approach that fit my goals and strategy.

Fast forward to 2026. I have spent the past six years working professionally in the industry, advising a hedge fund, working for a number of third-party research companies, and building a dividend growth portfolio that has become the foundation of a serious long-term investment strategy.

That strategy is built around what I like to call the “Big Picture” approach, as it combines macroeconomic research, structural trends, political developments, competitive business models, and durable wealth-building strategies like dividend growth investing.

My goal has always been to help investors filter out the daily market noise and focus on the factors that actually drive long-term returns. Even if some readers disagree with a specific stock pick or a thesis, I want them to walk away with a better understanding of the bigger picture that helps them in their own investment process.

Now, it's time to take all of this to the next level. I believe that everything I have done so far has led to this big moment, as I get to introduce my new endeavor called “Main Street Alpha,” which I'm launching here on Seeking Alpha with Albert Marko, my business partner, long-term mentor, and friend.

It's everything we have done over the past 10 years, packaged and brought to a whole new level, to bring institutional-grade research to the masses.

Why We Created Main Street Alpha

The current market environment seems to be louder and more challenging than ever.

Every day is a bombardment of news headlines, economic numbers, and a ton of narratives that have the potential to change everything happening in the markets. Essentially, the financial media cycle is moving faster, which is further worsened by social media-fueled noise and artificial intelligence. It has created a data overload.

In my personal experience, some of the most successful investors are the ones who filter out that noise, maintain a focus on the Big Picture (there it is again), and build portfolios that work for them. One thing a former mentor always told me was, “It’s all noise.”

That philosophy has always been the core of my work here on Seeking Alpha.

However, over time, it became increasingly clear to me that my readers wanted more than just ideas on single stocks. They wanted a place to discuss ideas in a more in-depth way, at a higher frequency, and with more in-depth analysis. And personally, I also wanted more. It was time for a change.

This reminds me of the following quote from the 1987 movie “Wall Street”:

If you're not inside, you are outside, OK?

We're going to change that, as we'll be that helping hand in a noisy market that helps you focus on the drivers that actually matter for long-term returns. To achieve that, we'll combine macroeconomic analysis, structural investment trends, and individual company research.

To us, the goal is very straightforward:

Help individual investors approach markets with the same big-picture mindset used by professional investors.

What You'll Find Inside Main Street Alpha

Main Street Alpha is built on a number of core pillars, all of which have the goal to help you make better long-term decisions.

First of all, our members will receive high-conviction investment ideas. All of these are fully supported by detailed research and Big-Picture analysis. Our research is designed to find the best companies and discuss major macro and geopolitical trends. Essentially, in our service, we go the extra mile and tell you what the “big guys” on Wall Street and in Washington, D.C., are discussing. Suddenly, you're an “insider” as well.

Second, we publish deep-dive research reports that go beyond the “traditional” articles you may be used to on the Internet. Our research deep-dives into various industries, macroeconomic developments, and niche investment opportunities that often go unnoticed.

Just think about the in-depth research I have written so far, taken to the next level.

Moreover, our members will get access to three portfolios:

  • Leo's personal portfolio.
  • A dividend income portfolio with a >5% average yield and high-single-digit annual dividend growth.
  • A community portfolio that is funded with service proceeds and directly influenced by members who get a say in what we buy and sell.

On top of that, members get full access to a private chat with direct access to me, the community, and any real-time investment alerts we publish.

In other words, we're going exclusive, to a whole new level, and you're invited!

Who's Behind Main Street Alpha

This service is run by Albert Marko and myself.

Most of you will be familiar with me. Not only did I just share my story with you, but I also spent the past 10 years sharing way too many details about my personal life. I'm sometimes surprised by how much people know about me and by how much people care.

Albert, however, is a name you may not be familiar with. Until now, of course.

I have worked with Albert for more than six years in various settings. He brings a different but highly complementary skill set, as he's a political-economic consultant and investor with roughly two decades of experience in foreign affairs and geopolitical analysis.

Moreover, throughout his career, he has advised hedge funds, corporations, and institutional investors on political risk, legislative developments, and international relations. His work has also involved consulting policymakers and members of Congress on geopolitical strategy and economic policy.

For a Big Picture approach, there's no better partner.

Hence, by integrating political analysis with macroeconomic research and equity strategy, Main Street Alpha aims to give investors a clearer view of the forces that truly drive long-term returns and generate long-term alpha and income for dividend investors.

Who Main Street Alpha Is For

This service is for everyone who has money invested or plans to invest.

However, to narrow it down, it's especially ideal for readers who:

  • Focus on long-term wealth creation.
  • Want to understand macro and geopolitical trends.
  • Appreciate dividend growth and income investing.
  • Prefer research-driven investment ideas.

If you're a short-term trader, this service may not be for you, as we help people achieve long-term goals and do not focus on short-term trading signals. However, even if you’re a trader and want to get a better understanding of the market, this service is for you as well.

After all, even traders need to understand the bigger picture.

A Quick Note on Expectations

Before wrapping things up, I want to make one more thing clear.

I'm a macro/equity strategist, not a salesman. This service is not about chasing the next hot stock or promising you quick gains. Markets do not work that way, and anyone who claims otherwise may be selling you something other than good research.

Some ideas will work extremely well. Others may take a while to unfold. And, occasionally, we'll be wrong on things. That's part of investing.

What matters is the process to increase the risk/reward and build something that works for you.

That's what we are about.

Join Us On The Next Stage Of The Journey

Over the past 10 years, the Seeking Alpha community has played a major role in shaping the way I think about markets and investing.

Main Street Alpha is the next step in the investment journey.

It's the place where we take everything we have been doing to a whole new level. Better intel, better research, portfolio management, and fruitful community discussions.

If that sounds like the investing approach you are looking for, we invite you to join us.

Main Street Alpha is available for $599 per year, with a limited launch discount for the first 400 members who will get the whole package for $499.

data.title: Bank Of America: Heading Into Q1 With More Priced In Than Left To Price
data.description:
The investment thesis for Bank of America (BAC) today seems to me to be leaning on the durability of net interest income growth in an environment that is beginning to change.

The bank continues to deliver solid results, with NII still healthy and resilient and a well-diversified business base. But the market has clearly stopped looking at what has been delivered recently and has started to question what is sustainable from now on. It is in this gap between perception and reality that I see the opportunity starting to form as the 1Q26 earnings approach (scheduled for April 15, before the opening bell).

At the same time, BAC no longer seems to me to be just a pure "spread bank," and this may not yet be well embedded in its share price. The evolution of fee businesses—wealth and capital markets, more specifically—suggests a more balanced model throughout the cycle and with the ability to cushion part of a normalization of the NII. This particular NII normalization, on the other hand, seems to me well embedded in BAC's share price.

I don't see BAC as an obvious or immediate bull case, but in a scenario where the worst already seems widely priced in, the setup starts to get more interesting for those who are willing to wait for this confirmation.

The Market Has Already Moved On

In the last earnings season, broken down by sub-segments, bank revenues came as follows, as depicted in the chart below.

It is interesting how there is a low correlation between the 4Q25 revenue performance and year-to-date stock returns of these large U.S. banks. In a way, it reinforces the idea that the market is already looking far beyond the recent results and focused on the durability of earnings in a seemingly shifting macro environment.

As ~59% of BAC's revenues come from the consumer and wealth management sides of the business, this is a bank with a greater exposure in consumer spending than other "Big Five" Banks of the U.S. economy. Thus, in a K-shaped economy, the reading for Bank of America becomes more nuanced, with arguably a slightly greater hint of uncertainty about earnings quality.

Reading the Macro Through BAC

In a bottom-up macro reading, the last Bank of America conference at RBC Capital Markets in March offered a macro reading, on the part of the bank, which I would classify as "resilient, but not clean".

The bank implicitly reiterates that the U.S. economy remains resilient in the aggregate, with healthy consumption and credit, but with clear signs of inequalities (K-shaped) and an environment that requires discipline and selectivity.

Starting with the still firm consumer. Spending has grown 5% YoY, with spending more focused on travel and entertainment, and with the consumer still with excess deposits (+13% vs. pre-COVID levels). This is not a slowing economy and not even close to a hard landing. The important nuance, however, is that in a K-shaped economy, high income spends more and low income is more pressured. On one end, this is connected with inflation, where part of the economy still has strong demand, and services inflation remains high (3.1% in February).

The credit market remains super healthy (for now) with average portfolio FICO at 780+, low exposure to bad credit, and no sign of relevant deterioration, also indicating the system is far from stress.

Business activity is still going on, according to early Q1 run-rate indicators. Lending "strong out the gate" with capital markets seeing IB growing 10% YoY and Markets in low double digits—no M&A freeze and no extreme risk aversion environment. Net interest income (NII) growing at 7% YoY and stable deposits show that interest rates are still high enough to benefit banks.

Implicitly, Bank of America's management team conveys an idea of how it reads the Fed that the economy does not need stimulus due to strong consumption, healthy credit, and growing lending. But it's also not overheating based on moderate spending growth and K-shaped Dynamics, which could eventually leave the Fed locked in for longer.

Building A More Balanced Earnings Model

Bank of America continues with the credit growth strategy, but in this case, selectively with high quality and underwriting refined by AI. No chasing growth. FY25 showed loans growing 8% YoY and deposits 3% YoY. CET1 came in at 11.4%, above the low.

The focus has clearly been on efficiency (146bps YoY improvement in efficiency ratio reaching 62%) and operating leverage, with guidance for FY26 of +200bps in operating leverage (currently at 2.5%), with frugality in costs reinforcing the view of a good environment, but not "easy money".

The strong bet seems to be on fee businesses. Wealth management has been growing, and capital markets have been active, hedging against any fall in NII in the future. Asset management fees grew 12% YoY, sales & trading up 11% YoY in FY25. IB fees and NII both growing by 7% YoY (and with a guidance of 5-7% in FY26) also show that Bank of America is already diversified.

Being historically a deposit-funded spread bank (and still trading at a multiple pegged to it), Bank of America has sought a model prepared for multiple interest rate scenarios. So much so that the strong NII model in high interest rates and strong fees for a low interest rate scenario/normalization seems to be playing out in practice, as ROE and ROTCE at 10.6% and 14.2% (solid profitability) were posted in FY25.

A Word Ahead of Q1

To beat the market estimates, Bank of America will need to post EPS numbers in excess of $1.00 (which would be 11.3% YoY growth) and revenues in excess of $29.77 billion (which would be 8.8% YoY growth).

The consensus looks slightly constructive over the past three months, especially in terms of earnings, having revised that figure upwards by ~1%. Precisely the NII guidance coming better than feared and rate cuts not yet hitting full in the P&L are arguably the main drivers of this slight optimism for 1Q26.

I would also say that the BAC reiterating that stronger Markets and IB at the beginning of Q1 also looks like something that surprises positively, since the market was afraid of a slowdown.

But the consensus being revised up by just 1% for 1Q26 shows only slightly less bearish. The drop in BAC's market cap by 10% from January to March 6 seems to me to be an indication of the market devaluing the peak of the NII (7.2% YoY growth in 2025), with the 2026 guidance, although strong, showing normalization at the end-point.

That being said, I believe that looking at 1Q26 and trying to find answers to BAC's rate sensitivity (in this case, still ~$2 billion impact per 100bps), whether additional hedges or stickier deposits could make this more constructive. In this case, I would pay attention to the fee businesses (where the hedge needs to prove) with wealth and Markets managing to sustain the growth of double digits.

Finally, BAC comes to report earnings trading as a kind of "run-of-the-mill" at 11.3x forward earnings (and a long-term PEG of 0.86, attractive) and also at 1.28x book value—all numbers similar to industry averages.

Risks and Final Remarks

I don't think the timing is an obvious "pound the table" moment for a strong bull case in BAC.

Shares are trading weakly, but also very clearly reflecting a wait-and-see setup. This is the case as we can see from the 2-year Treasury at 3.8%, the 10-year at 4.33%, and the 10y-2y curve at +0.51 p.p.

That is, the curve is already positively sloped, and possibly no longer inverted in this stretch. This suggests that the market is not pricing in further strong tightening from the Fed, but it is also not buying a large immediate monetary easing.

In any case, I think the BAC may post a relatively safe quarter, but the central point remains the NII's trajectory throughout 2026 and the bank's ability to sustain growth via fee businesses in a potentially lower interest rate environment.

The good news is that in terms of valuations, Bank of America already looks largely "de-risked" with trailing multiples well aligned with its historical averages after recently testing P/B levels close to 1.5x. On the one hand, the suggestion is that the compression of multiples may have already occurred and embeds the normalization of the NII, reflecting well the "good but not great" environment.

Lastly, the opposite of this scenario is the main risk of the BAC thesis. I would argue that the fact that its earnings remain more rate-dependent than the market is comfortable with, i.e., that the NII slows faster than expected and other engines fail to compensate, may cause a prolongation of the bearish streak for Bank of America's shares.

I don't believe this is the playbook at the moment, and I see the risk as more balanced from current levels. Therefore, I reiterate a moderately constructive view and my Buy recommendation for Bank of America.

data.title: Fed Walks A Policy Tightrope As Iran Conflict Clouds The Outlook
data.description:
The Federal Reserve is steering through one of the most challenging policy environments in years as the war with Iran destabilizes global energy markets and raises uncertainty about inflation and economic activity. The spike in geopolitical volatility leaves policymakers navigating an exceptionally narrow path: tightening too aggressively risks tipping the economy into recession, while easing too soon could reignite inflation. For the near term, the least worst option is to sit tight and leave rates unchanged until the incoming data start to make a strong case for changing the monetary policy bias.

The Cleveland Federal Reserve president, Beth Hammack, underscored this point in an interview with AP on Monday. Noting that keeping the Fed’s target rate steady for the near term “for quite some time” is her preference, “I can foresee scenarios where we would need to reduce rates... if the labor market deteriorates significantly,” she added. “Or I could see where we might need to raise rates if inflation stays persistently above our target.”

The Treasury market has recently recalibrated its outlook and is now firmly pricing in a higher probability of a rate hike in the near term after an extended run of dovish pricing. The policy-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y)(3.84% as of Apr. 6) continues to trade above the median effective Fed funds rate (3.64%), marking a clear change in sentiment toward a hawkish bias for the first time since 2022.

The degree of future inflation risk remains debatable, as does the potential for a slowdown in economic growth. But the odds are widely perceived as higher for one or both sides of this coin.

“All roads now lead to higher prices and slower growth,” predicts IMF managing director Kristalina Georgieva. “We are in a world of elevated uncertainty,” she told Reuters yesterday, pointing to a variety of risk factors, including geopolitical tensions, technological advancements, climate shocks and demographic shifts. “All of this means that after we recover from this shock, we need to keep our eyes open for the next one.”

Current Fed policy is still modestly tight, based on a simple model using the unemployment rate and the year-over-year change in the headline Consumer Price Index. That gives the central bank space to take a wait-and-see approach and monitor incoming numbers.

Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee, however, suggests that a rate hike could be near. Asked to characterize the state of risk for the economic outlook using a four-color template, ranging from “the house is on fire” red to “everything is looking swell” green, he responded. “At least orange. Orange with ​a chance of meatballs; it hasn’t been great.”

The Treasury market is inclined to agree lately. But inflation and economic data arrive with a lag, and so the Fed will continue to wait to develop a clearer view of how the economy’s reacting to the war with Iran.

The challenge is not waiting too long, lest inflation and/or slower growth move too far ahead of policy, which would leave the Fed in the difficult position of trying to play catch-up. That was the case when the central bank was slow to raise rates when inflation spiked in 2021-2022, and so avoiding that mistake is very much a priority just a few years after that policy mistake.

At the same time, the risk of acting too early could make a bad situation worse, on either the inflation or growth fronts.

In the end, the Fed’s challenge is less about choosing the perfect policy setting and more about staying nimble in a world where the ground keeps shifting. The only certainty is that the next move—whenever it comes—will be made under conditions that are anything but certain.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
